subject: family_law
topic_heading: Federal Child Support Guidelines — Table Amount
subtopic_heading: Table Amount — Payor Income and Number of Children
fact_pattern: Daniel and Fiona separated and have two children, aged 8 and 11, who live primarily with Fiona. Daniel earns $90,000 per year gross income in Ontario. There is no shared parenting arrangement. Both parties agree that the Federal Child Support Guidelines apply.
call_of_question: Under the Federal Child Support Guidelines, what is the starting point for determining the amount of child support Daniel must pay?

options.A: The court has broad discretion to set any amount based on the children's needs and Daniel's ability to pay, without reference to a table.
options.B: Child support is calculated as a fixed percentage of Daniel's gross income regardless of the number of children.
options.C: The starting point is the table amount specified in Schedule I of the Federal Child Support Guidelines for a payor residing in Ontario with a gross annual income of $90,000 and two children; this is the presumptive amount and is treated as the floor, not a ceiling.
options.D: Child support is set at $500 per child per month as a statutory flat rate for parents earning between $75,000 and $100,000.

explanation: Under Federal Child Support Guidelines s.3(1)(a), where a child resides primarily with one parent, the amount of a child support order for that parent is the applicable table amount for the payor's province of residence, based on the payor's annual income and the number of children. The table amounts in Schedule I are the presumptive starting point and are treated as the appropriate amount in the absence of factors justifying departure. There is no fixed flat rate or open-ended judicial discretion to ignore the table amount.
why_A_wrong: The Federal Child Support Guidelines eliminate open-ended judicial discretion for the base child support amount. The table amount under FCSG s.3(1)(a) is presumptive and must be awarded unless statutory exceptions apply.
why_B_wrong: The table amounts are not calculated as a simple fixed percentage. They vary by province, number of children, and income level, and are set out in Schedule I of the Guidelines.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FCSG s.3(1)(a) and Schedule I table amounts (Ontario, $90,000 income, 2 children) provide the presumptive child support amount.
why_D_wrong: No statutory flat rate of $500 per child exists. The FCSG table amounts are graduated by income and vary by province; there is no flat rate for any income band.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Federal Child Support Guidelines — Table Amount
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 3(1)(a), Schedule I

subject: family_law
topic_heading: Federal Child Support Guidelines — Table Amount
subtopic_heading: Determination of Income for FCSG Purposes
fact_pattern: Victor is self-employed operating a consulting business. His corporation reports pre-tax income of $200,000, but Victor pays himself a salary of $80,000 and reinvests the remaining $120,000 in the corporation. He argues that child support should be based only on his $80,000 salary. His former spouse argues that the court should look beyond the corporate salary.
call_of_question: How is Victor's income determined for purposes of the Federal Child Support Guidelines when he is a self-employed shareholder of a private corporation?

options.A: Victor's income is his declared salary of $80,000; the court cannot look behind the corporate structure.
options.B: Victor's income is the pre-tax corporate income of $200,000 because the Guidelines always attribute the entire corporate income to the shareholder-payor.
options.C: Under FCSG Schedule III and s.18, where a payor controls a private corporation, the court may include in income amounts that the corporation earned but did not distribute as salary, where those amounts are not required for business operations and the retention constitutes an artificial reduction of the payor's income.
options.D: Victor's income is determined by averaging his salary over the last five years, ignoring corporate profits entirely.

explanation: Under Federal Child Support Guidelines s.18(1), where a paying spouse controls a corporation, the court may determine the spouse's annual income to include the pre-tax income of the corporation, less any amounts that are reasonably required for the operation of the business. This prevents artificial suppression of income by retaining earnings in a corporation. The court does not automatically attribute all corporate income, but will look behind the salary when the retention of earnings is not required for business purposes and results in an artificial understatement of the payor's income.
why_A_wrong: Courts regularly look behind corporate structures when a payor controls the corporation. FCSG s.18 specifically addresses this situation and permits the court to include undistributed corporate income in the payor's income for Guidelines purposes.
why_B_wrong: The court does not automatically attribute the entire pre-tax corporate income to the payor. Under s.18, only amounts not reasonably required for business operations may be added back. Amounts genuinely reinvested for business purposes may be excluded.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FCSG s.18 allows the court to include undistributed corporate income where the payor controls the corporation and the retention is not required for legitimate business purposes.
why_D_wrong: Income averaging over five years may be used in appropriate circumstances under FCSG Schedule III, but it does not eliminate the court's ability to consider corporate income under s.18. The five-year averaging approach is not the default rule for self-employed payors with private corporations.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Determination of Income — FCSG s.18 and Schedule III
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 18(1), Schedule III

subject: family_law
topic_heading: Special and Extraordinary Expenses — FCSG s.7
subtopic_heading: Categories of Special and Extraordinary Expenses Under FCSG s.7
fact_pattern: Olivia and Steve have one child, aged 7, who lives primarily with Olivia. Steve pays the table amount of child support. Olivia now asks the court to order Steve to contribute to the following additional expenses: (1) daycare costs of $1,200/month while Olivia works; (2) the child's hockey equipment and registration fees of $3,000/year; (3) private school tuition of $15,000/year. Steve argues none of these are s.7 expenses.
call_of_question: Which of these three items is/are properly characterized as special or extraordinary expenses under FCSG s.7?

options.A: None — s.7 expenses are limited to medical and dental costs; daycare, sports, and school tuition are not covered.
options.B: Daycare costs and private school tuition are s.7 expenses; recreational sports expenses are not s.7 expenses under any circumstances.
options.C: All three may qualify as s.7 expenses: daycare (s.7(1)(a) — child care required by employment), private school tuition (s.7(1)(d) — education programs necessary for the child or exceeding what is reasonably expected at the public school level); hockey may qualify as an extraordinary expense under s.7(1)(f) if it is an 'extraordinary' extracurricular activity given the parties' circumstances.
options.D: Only private school tuition qualifies because s.7 is limited to educational expenses above the public school level.

explanation: Federal Child Support Guidelines s.7(1) lists categories of special or extraordinary expenses: (a) child care required by employment/illness/disability; (b) medical/dental insurance premiums; (c) medical/dental expenses exceeding insurance; (d) education programs (including post-secondary and programs necessary for the child's needs); (e) post-secondary education expenses; (f) extraordinary extracurricular activities. Child care while Olivia works is clearly s.7(1)(a). Private school tuition may qualify under s.7(1)(d). Hockey registration may qualify as an extraordinary extracurricular activity under s.7(1)(f), assessed in the context of the parties' incomes and historical activities. All three potentially qualify; the court applies a proportionality test under s.7(2) based on income.
why_A_wrong: Section 7 is not limited to medical and dental costs. It expressly covers child care (s.7(1)(a)), educational programs (s.7(1)(d)), and extraordinary extracurricular activities (s.7(1)(f)), among other categories.
why_B_wrong: Extracurricular activities can qualify as extraordinary s.7 expenses under s.7(1)(f) where they are extraordinary given the family's means and lifestyle. It is incorrect to categorically exclude sports from s.7.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. All three items potentially qualify under specific s.7 subsections, and each is allocated between the parties in proportion to their incomes under s.7(2).
why_D_wrong: Section 7 covers much more than education above the public school level. It includes child care, medical expenses, and extracurricular activities, among other categories.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Special and Extraordinary Expenses — FCSG s.7
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 7(1)(a), s. 7(1)(d), s. 7(1)(f), s. 7(2)

subject: family_law
topic_heading: Special and Extraordinary Expenses — FCSG s.7
subtopic_heading: Apportionment of Section 7 Expenses Between Parents
fact_pattern: Diane earns $120,000 per year and her former husband Carl earns $80,000 per year. Their daughter participates in competitive gymnastics with annual costs of $6,000. The court has determined this qualifies as an extraordinary extracurricular expense under FCSG s.7(1)(f). Carl asks whether he must pay half of the $6,000.
call_of_question: How are s.7 expenses apportioned between the parties under the Federal Child Support Guidelines?

options.A: Section 7 expenses are always split 50/50 between the parents regardless of their incomes.
options.B: Section 7 expenses are borne entirely by the parent in whose primary care the child lives.
options.C: Under FCSG s.7(2), s.7 expenses are apportioned between the parents in proportion to their respective incomes after deducting any contribution from the child (e.g., scholarships); on these facts, Diane bears 60% and Carl bears 40% of the $6,000.
options.D: The parent who earns more is solely responsible for all s.7 expenses; Carl owes nothing because Diane earns more.

explanation: Federal Child Support Guidelines s.7(2) provides that the parents must contribute to s.7 expenses in proportion to their respective incomes, after deducting any contribution from the child. Diane's income of $120,000 represents 60% of the combined income of $200,000; Carl's $80,000 represents 40%. Therefore, Diane contributes $3,600 and Carl contributes $2,400 of the annual $6,000 gymnastics cost. The proportionate sharing principle, not a 50/50 split or winner-takes-all approach, governs s.7 apportionment.
why_A_wrong: Section 7 expenses are not split equally. FCSG s.7(2) requires proportional contribution based on each parent's income. Equal splitting would only arise where incomes happen to be equal.
why_B_wrong: The parent in whose primary care the child lives is not solely responsible for s.7 expenses. Both parents contribute proportionally under s.7(2), regardless of primary residence.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FCSG s.7(2) requires proportional contribution: Diane (60%) and Carl (40%) based on their relative incomes of $120,000 and $80,000.
why_D_wrong: The higher-earning parent is not solely responsible for s.7 expenses. Both parents share the obligation proportionally. The higher-earning parent simply pays a larger proportionate share.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Section 7 Expenses — Apportionment
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 7(2)

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Imputing Income — Purposeful Underemployment and Related Circumstances
fact_pattern: Brian, who has a law degree and five years of litigation experience, voluntarily resigned from his position as an associate earning $130,000/year the day before a child support motion. He claims he is now self-employed doing occasional legal consulting, earning $20,000/year. His former spouse seeks an imputation of income. Brian argues the court must accept his actual income.
call_of_question: Under FCSG s.19, in what circumstances may a court impute income to a child support payor, and would Brian's circumstances warrant imputation?

options.A: Courts may only impute income in cases of criminal fraud; resignation from employment, however suspicious the timing, cannot give rise to imputation.
options.B: Courts may impute income under s.19 where a spouse is intentionally under-employed or unemployed; however, Brian's right to choose his career path means no imputation is possible.
options.C: Under FCSG s.19(1)(a), a court may impute income where a spouse is intentionally under-employed or unemployed, unless the situation is appropriate given the circumstances (such as illness, disability, or reasonable career transition); Brian's sudden voluntary resignation to near-zero income the day before a child support motion, without legitimate justification, strongly supports imputation at a level reflecting his earning capacity.
options.D: The court must average Brian's income over the last three years and use that as his income for child support purposes; imputation is not available.

explanation: Federal Child Support Guidelines s.19(1)(a) permits a court to impute income to a paying spouse where the spouse is intentionally under-employed or unemployed, unless the situation is reasonable in the circumstances (e.g., disability, illness, or a genuine and reasonable career change). Brian's voluntary resignation the day before a child support motion, combined with his legal qualifications and abrupt shift to $20,000 consulting income, are strong indicators of intentional income reduction for purposes of avoiding child support. Courts regularly impute income based on earning capacity in such circumstances.
why_A_wrong: Income imputation under FCSG s.19 is not limited to criminal fraud. Intentional underemployment — including voluntary resignation for reasons connected to avoiding child support — is a recognized and common basis for imputation.
why_B_wrong: While individuals have the right to choose their careers, the right to change employment does not insulate a payor from income imputation where the change is found to be intentional underemployment under s.19(1)(a). Courts assess the bona fides and reasonableness of the career change.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FCSG s.19(1)(a) authorizes imputation where intentional underemployment exists without legitimate justification; Brian's suspicious timing and dramatic income reduction strongly warrant imputation.
why_D_wrong: Three-year income averaging (available under Schedule III) is a tool for dealing with variable income, not an alternative to imputation in cases of deliberate underemployment. Imputation under s.19 is available and appropriate here.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Imputing Income — FCSG s.19
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 19(1)(a)

subject: family_law
topic_heading: Child Support in Shared Parenting Arrangements
subtopic_heading: Shared Parenting Over 40% — Offset Method Under FCSG s.9
fact_pattern: Anna earns $100,000/year and Mark earns $60,000/year. They have one child, and pursuant to a parenting order, the child spends 45% of the time with Mark and 55% with Anna. Under the Ontario table, the table amount for Mark's income of $60,000 for one child is $572/month; for Anna's income of $100,000, it is $879/month. Both parents seek to pay the minimum child support possible.
call_of_question: How does FCSG s.9 govern child support when the child spends at least 40% of the time with each parent?

options.A: When a child spends more than 40% of the time with each parent, no child support is payable by either party; the arrangement is presumed financially neutral.
options.B: FCSG s.9 creates a mandatory formula requiring the higher-earning parent to pay the lower-earning parent the full table amount.
options.C: Under FCSG s.9, where a child spends at least 40% of the time with each parent, the court must consider the table amounts for each parent, the increased cost of the shared arrangement, and the condition, means, needs, and other circumstances of each parent; the offset method (table amount for Anna minus table amount for Mark = $879 - $572 = $307/month payable by Anna) is a common starting point, but the court may adjust up or down.
options.D: Section 9 applies only where the parenting time is exactly 50/50; at 55/45 the standard table amount applies.

explanation: Federal Child Support Guidelines s.9 applies where a spouse exercises parenting time with a child for not less than 40% of the time over the course of a year. In that case, the court shall take into account: (a) the amounts set out in the applicable tables for each parent; (b) the increased costs of shared parenting; and (c) the condition, means, needs and other circumstances of each spouse and the children. The offset method — comparing the table amounts and paying the difference — is frequently used as a starting point (here, $879 - $572 = $307 payable by Anna), but it is not mandatory and the court retains discretion to depart from it (see Contino v. Leonelli-Contino, [2005] 3 SCR 217).
why_A_wrong: Section 9 does not eliminate child support obligations in shared parenting arrangements. The court must conduct a three-part analysis; child support is rarely zero even in 50/50 arrangements unless incomes are equal.
why_B_wrong: FCSG s.9 does not create a mandatory formula requiring the higher earner to pay the full table amount. The three-factor test in s.9 — including the offset method as a starting point — governs the analysis.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FCSG s.9 applies whenever parenting time reaches 40% or more, and the three-factor test (table amounts, increased costs, circumstances) applies; the offset is a common starting point per Contino.
why_D_wrong: Section 9 applies at 40% or more, not only at exactly 50/50. At 45% parenting time, s.9 is engaged. The 40% threshold triggers the full s.9 analysis.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Shared Parenting — FCSG s.9
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 9; Contino v. Leonelli-Contino, [2005] 3 SCR 217

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Child Support for Adult Child in Post-Secondary Education
fact_pattern: Sophie is 21 years old and enrolled in a four-year university program. Her parents divorced when she was 10. Her father argues that his child support obligation ended when Sophie turned 18 and he is no longer required to pay. Her mother applies for an order of continued child support.
call_of_question: Under the Divorce Act and Federal Child Support Guidelines, can a child support order be made or continued for an adult child attending post-secondary education?

options.A: No — child support is available only for children under 18 in Canada; turning 18 terminates all child support obligations automatically.
options.B: Yes — under DA s.2(1), the definition of 'child of the marriage' includes a child who is 18 or older and unable by reason of illness, disability, or other cause to withdraw from parental charge; attendance at university is recognized as an 'other cause' preventing withdrawal from parental charge, and Sophie may qualify.
options.C: Yes — but only until Sophie turns 21; after age 21, child support for post-secondary students is unavailable.
options.D: Yes — but support for adult post-secondary students is capped at $1,000/month regardless of the payor's income.

explanation: Divorce Act s.2(1) defines 'child of the marriage' to include a person who is 18 or older and 'is unable, by reason of illness, disability, or other cause, to withdraw from their charge or to obtain the necessaries of life.' Courts have consistently held that full-time post-secondary attendance is an 'other cause' within this definition, preventing a young adult from withdrawing from parental charge. Child support for post-secondary students is calculated differently from table amounts — the court looks at the child's actual expenses, the parent's ability to pay, and any contribution the child can make (FCSG s.3(2)(b)). There is no automatic cutoff at age 21 and no fixed cap.
why_A_wrong: The Divorce Act does not impose a hard age-18 cutoff for child support. Section 2(1) expressly extends the definition of 'child of the marriage' to adults who cannot withdraw from parental charge, which includes post-secondary students.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. DA s.2(1) extends child support to adults in post-secondary education as a recognized 'other cause'; FCSG s.3(2)(b) governs quantum for such children.
why_C_wrong: There is no age-21 statutory cutoff for adult child support in Canada. Support may continue beyond age 21 where the child remains legitimately unable to withdraw from parental charge.
why_D_wrong: No statutory cap of $1,000/month applies to adult post-secondary child support. Quantum is assessed on the particular facts including the child's expenses, both parents' income, and any contribution the child makes.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Adult Children — Post-Secondary Education and Child Support
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 2(1); Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 3(2)(b)

tested_concepts: DA s.2(1) definition child of marriage

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Variation of Child Support — Material Change and Contino
fact_pattern: A child support order was made two years ago based on the payor's income of $75,000. The payor's income has now increased to $100,000. The recipient applies to vary the order to increase the table amount. The payor argues that the income increase alone is insufficient to constitute a 'material change in circumstances.'
call_of_question: What is the standard for varying a child support order under the Divorce Act, and does an increase in the payor's income satisfy it?

options.A: The payor is correct; a change in income alone is never a material change because the Guidelines produce a new table amount automatically without a court order.
options.B: Child support orders can be varied only if the child's needs have changed, not based solely on changes to the payor's income.
options.C: Under DA s.17(4) and s.17(6.1), a change in the condition, means, needs or other circumstances of either spouse or a child constitutes a material change warranting variation; an increase in the payor's income is itself a change in the payor's 'means' and is a recognized basis for upward variation of the table amount.
options.D: The increase from $75,000 to $100,000 is too modest to qualify as material; the income must increase by at least 25% before variation is available.

explanation: Under Divorce Act s.17(4), a court may vary a child support order where there has been a change in the condition, means, needs or other circumstances of the children or the spouse since the order was made. Under s.17(6.1), where the change is in income of the payor, the variation is made by recalculating the applicable table amount. A change in the payor's income is expressly recognized as a change in 'means' sufficient to constitute a material change, entitling the court to update the child support amount. There is no 25% minimum threshold.
why_A_wrong: Child support orders do not adjust automatically with income changes. A variation application to the court is required to obtain an order reflecting the new income. The Guidelines do not self-execute without a court order.
why_B_wrong: A change in the payor's income is expressly recognized by DA s.17(4) as a change in the payor's 'means' warranting variation. The child's needs need not have changed; the payor's improved means is independently sufficient.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. DA s.17(4) and s.17(6.1) recognize a change in the payor's income as a material change warranting variation; the table amount is then recalculated.
why_D_wrong: No 25% threshold exists in the Divorce Act or the Federal Child Support Guidelines. Any genuine, non-trivial change in income constitutes a change in 'means' for the purposes of s.17(4).

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Variation of Child Support — DA s.17
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 17(4), s. 17(6.1); Contino v. Leonelli-Contino, [2005] 3 SCR 217

tested_concepts: payor income change as material change

subject: family_law
topic_heading: Shared Parenting — 40% Threshold for FCSG s.9
subtopic_heading: 40% Threshold — What Counts as Parenting Time for FCSG s.9
fact_pattern: A parenting order grants the mother 210 overnights per year and the father 155 overnights per year with their one child. The father argues that because he has the child for approximately 42% of overnights, FCSG s.9 applies and he should pay a reduced amount of child support rather than the full table amount. The mother argues the full table amount applies because s.9 should be calculated differently.
call_of_question: For the purpose of FCSG s.9, how is the 40% threshold calculated, and does the father meet it?

options.A: The 40% threshold is calculated based only on daytime hours, not overnights; 155 overnights is insufficient without counting daytime hours.
options.B: The 40% threshold under FCSG s.9 is measured over the course of a year; a parent who exercises parenting time for at least 40% of the time (approximately 146 days or more out of 365) meets the threshold; 155 overnights represents approximately 42% and satisfies the threshold.
options.C: The 40% threshold requires exactly 50/50 parenting time; anything less requires payment of the full table amount.
options.D: FCSG s.9 applies only if the child sleeps at each parent's home an equal number of nights; at 155 vs 210 overnights, s.9 is inapplicable.

explanation: Federal Child Support Guidelines s.9 applies where 'a spouse exercises parenting time with a child for not less than 40 percent of the time over the course of a year.' The threshold is 40% — not 50%. Courts typically calculate the percentage based on the total number of days (or nights) in a year attributable to each parent. At 155 overnights out of 365 (approximately 42.5%), the father meets the s.9 threshold, and the court must apply the three-factor analysis from Contino v. Leonelli-Contino, [2005] 3 SCR 217 rather than simply imposing the full table amount. The calculation is not limited to overnights if there is significant daytime-only time, but overnights are the most common metric used.
why_A_wrong: Courts most commonly measure parenting time for FCSG s.9 purposes using overnights as a practical proxy for total time spent with each parent. Daytime hours can be included where relevant, but overnights are the standard measure; 155 overnights clearly exceeds 40% of the year.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FCSG s.9 threshold is 40% or more of the year; 155 out of 365 days equals approximately 42.5%, satisfying the threshold and engaging the three-factor Contino analysis.
why_C_wrong: FCSG s.9 does not require equal (50/50) parenting time. The threshold is 40%, not 50%. Any parenting time at or above 40% engages s.9.
why_D_wrong: FCSG s.9 does not require equal overnight distribution. The 40% threshold can be met at any split above 40%, including 42% vs 58% as in this scenario.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Shared Parenting 40% Threshold — FCSG s.9
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 9; Contino v. Leonelli-Contino, [2005] 3 SCR 217

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: FCSG s.3(2)(b) — Quantum for Adult Children Over Age of Majority
fact_pattern: Noah, aged 20, is enrolled full-time at university. His parents divorced when he was 12. A court order made at that time required his father to pay the table amount of child support. His mother now applies to vary the order to reflect Noah's current university expenses including tuition of $8,000/year, residence of $12,000/year, and books of $1,500/year. The father argues the table amount continues to apply.
call_of_question: Under FCSG s.3(2)(b), how is child support calculated for a child over the age of majority (such as Noah)?

options.A: The table amount continues to apply unchanged because it is the presumptive amount for all children regardless of age.
options.B: Under FCSG s.3(2)(b), where a child is 18 or older, the court may look at the condition, means, needs, and other circumstances of the child and both spouses in determining an appropriate amount of child support; actual expenses of university attendance replace the table amount as the starting point.
options.C: No child support is available for children over 18 regardless of whether they are in school.
options.D: Child support for adult children must equal the table amount regardless of the child's actual expenses.

explanation: Federal Child Support Guidelines s.3(2)(b) provides that where a child is the age of majority or over, if the court is satisfied that the child is unable to withdraw from their parents' charge for specified reasons (such as full-time post-secondary attendance), the court may use the table amount or it may make an order that it considers appropriate having regard to the condition, means, needs, and other circumstances of the child and the spouses. For a university student living away from home, courts regularly use Noah's actual educational expenses as the starting point rather than the table amount, and apportion these expenses between the parents in proportion to their incomes under an approach analogous to s.7.
why_A_wrong: FCSG s.3(2)(b) expressly provides an alternative approach for children over the age of majority: the court may consider actual circumstances and expenses rather than applying the table amount as the mandatory floor.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FCSG s.3(2)(b) gives the court discretion to use the table amount or a different amount based on the child's actual needs and expenses for children over the age of majority.
why_C_wrong: Divorce Act s.2(1) extends the definition of 'child of the marriage' to include adult children unable to withdraw from parental charge, including post-secondary students. Support is available for such children.
why_D_wrong: FCSG s.3(2)(b) gives the court flexibility to depart from the table amount for adult children. Actual expenses are the typical starting point for university students living away from home.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Adult Child Support — FCSG s.3(2)(b)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 3(2)(b); Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 2(1)

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Retroactive Child Support — DBS v SRG Factors
fact_pattern: Aaron and Bianca separated six years ago. A child support order was made at that time based on Aaron's income of $60,000. Aaron's income has since increased to $100,000, but he never voluntarily disclosed this to Bianca. Bianca learned of Aaron's income increase only recently and now brings a variation application seeking retroactive child support for the past four years.
call_of_question: Under the framework in DBS v SRG, [2006] 2 SCR 231, what factors govern whether and how far back a retroactive child support award should be made?

options.A: Retroactive child support is not available; child support can only be increased from the date of the variation application.
options.B: Under DBS v SRG, a court may award retroactive child support, taking into account: the reason for the recipient's delay in seeking support; the conduct of the payor (including non-disclosure of income); the circumstances of the child; and hardship to the payor from retroactive payment.
options.C: Retroactive child support is automatically awarded for the full limitation period of six years before the application.
options.D: Retroactive child support may only be awarded for one year prior to the application, with no exceptions.

explanation: In DBS v SRG, [2006] 2 SCR 231, the Supreme Court of Canada established the analytical framework for retroactive child support. The four factors are: (1) whether the recipient had a reasonable excuse for the delay in bringing the application; (2) whether the payor engaged in blameworthy conduct (such as non-disclosure of income increases); (3) the circumstances of the child and whether they will benefit from a retroactive award; and (4) whether the payor would suffer hardship from the retroactive award. The Court also established a general 'rule of thumb' that retroactive awards should not generally extend more than three years before the date of the formal claim, but this may be exceeded where there is payor misconduct (such as non-disclosure). Aaron's consistent non-disclosure of income is blameworthy conduct supporting a more generous retroactive award.
why_A_wrong: Retroactive child support is available in Canadian family law and was confirmed by the Supreme Court in DBS v SRG. Courts regularly award retroactive support where justified by the four-factor analysis.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. DBS v SRG establishes four factors: delay reason, payor blameworthy conduct, child's circumstances, and hardship to payor; non-disclosure supports a retroactive award.
why_C_wrong: There is no automatic full-limitation-period retroactivity. Courts apply the four-factor DBS analysis and generally apply a three-year presumptive period, which may be extended for payor misconduct.
why_D_wrong: A one-year limit on retroactive child support is not the rule established in DBS v SRG. The general starting point is three years before the formal claim, subject to extension for blameworthy payor conduct.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Retroactive Child Support — DBS v SRG
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: DBS v SRG; LJW v TAR; Henry v Henry; Hiemstra v Hiemstra, [2006] 2 SCR 231

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Tax Treatment — Child Support Payments Not Deductible/Not Income
fact_pattern: Kwame pays $1,500/month in child support to his former spouse Amara for their two children. Kwame's accountant advises him that he may deduct child support payments from his income for tax purposes. Amara's accountant advises her to include the child support received as taxable income. Both accountants are advising based on older rules.
call_of_question: What is the correct current tax treatment of child support payments in Canada?

options.A: Child support payments are deductible by the payor and taxable income to the recipient, as they have always been.
options.B: Child support payments made pursuant to a post-April 1997 order or agreement are neither deductible by the payor nor includible as income by the recipient; they are treated as tax-neutral.
options.C: Child support payments are taxable income to the recipient but not deductible by the payor.
options.D: Child support payments are deductible by the payor but not taxable income to the recipient.

explanation: Following the 1997 amendments to the Income Tax Act, child support payments made pursuant to a written agreement or court order made after April 30, 1997 are neither deductible by the payor nor includible as income by the recipient. This 'tax-neutral' treatment replaced the prior regime (under which child support was deductible by the payor and taxable to the recipient). The Federal Child Support Guidelines table amounts were set with this tax-neutral treatment in mind. Both accountants are relying on the pre-1997 rules, which no longer apply to post-April 1997 orders and agreements.
why_A_wrong: The deductible/includible regime applied only to child support agreements and orders made before May 1, 1997. The 1997 federal budget and Income Tax Act amendments changed this to tax-neutral treatment for subsequent orders.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Child support paid under post-April 1997 orders/agreements is neither deductible by the payor nor taxable income to the recipient — the 'Commencement Day' rule under the Income Tax Act makes post-1997 child support tax-neutral.
why_C_wrong: The asymmetric treatment of taxable to recipient but not deductible by payor has never been the Canadian rule. Post-1997, child support is fully tax-neutral — neither deductible nor taxable.
why_D_wrong: The pre-1997 regime was deductible by the payor and taxable to the recipient — not the reverse described in this option. Post-1997, neither deduction nor income inclusion applies.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Tax Treatment of Child Support Payments
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Income Tax Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 1 (5th Supp.), ss. 56(1)(b), 60(b) (Commencement Day rules post-1997)

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Undue Hardship Claim — Criteria Under FCSG s.10
fact_pattern: Derek earns $75,000/year and has two children from his current marriage who live with him. The Federal Child Support Guidelines table amount for his two children from his former marriage would be $1,200/month. He argues that paying $1,200/month in addition to supporting his new family creates undue hardship and he should pay less. His former spouse disputes this.
call_of_question: Under FCSG s.10, what must Derek establish to succeed on an undue hardship claim, and what additional requirement must be satisfied?

options.A: Derek must prove that paying the table amount would leave him with less than $500/month for personal expenses.
options.B: Under FCSG s.10, the court may award a different amount where the table amount would cause undue hardship to either spouse; Derek must: (1) establish one of the specified circumstances in s.10(2) (including unusually high debts or legal duties to support another person); and (2) show that his household standard of living is lower than the recipient's household standard of living.
options.C: Derek's new family obligations automatically establish undue hardship, requiring a reduction of child support.
options.D: Undue hardship is established solely by showing that the table amount exceeds 20% of the payor's income.

explanation: Federal Child Support Guidelines s.10 provides that a court may award a different amount where the table amount would cause undue hardship to the paying or receiving spouse. Two cumulative elements must be established: (1) one of the circumstances listed in s.10(2), which includes unusually high debts from the marriage, unusually high expenses for exercising parenting time, and legal duties to support another person (such as children from a subsequent relationship); and (2) the household standard of living test — the court will not grant an undue hardship claim if the payor's household standard of living is higher than the recipient's. Derek's support obligations to his new family may qualify under s.10(2)(d) (legal duty to support other children), but he must also show his household standard of living is not higher than his former spouse's.
why_A_wrong: No $500/month threshold test exists in FCSG s.10. The undue hardship analysis requires establishing a listed circumstance in s.10(2) and passing the household standard of living comparison test.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FCSG s.10 requires two cumulative elements: a s.10(2) listed circumstance and a lower household standard of living than the recipient's; both must be met.
why_C_wrong: New family obligations do not automatically establish undue hardship. They are one of the listed circumstances (s.10(2)(d)) but are only sufficient if the household standard of living test is also satisfied.
why_D_wrong: No 20% income threshold test is found in FCSG s.10. The undue hardship test is qualitative and requires analysis of specified circumstances and the comparative household standard of living test.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Undue Hardship — FCSG s.10
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 10(1), s. 10(2)

tested_concepts: FCSG s.10 undue hardship two-part test

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Step-Parent Obligation to Pay Child Support — FCSG s.5
fact_pattern: Helen and George cohabited for 6 years and then separated. During those 6 years, George treated Helen's 8-year-old daughter from a previous relationship as his own child — paying for her school activities, taking her to appointments, and acting as a parental figure. George has no biological children. Helen now seeks child support from George for her daughter. George argues he has no legal obligation to pay support because he is not the child's biological father.
call_of_question: Under what legal basis might George be obligated to pay child support for Helen's daughter, and what standard governs?

options.A: George has no obligation; only biological parents or legal adoptive parents are required to pay child support.
options.B: Under FCSG s.5 and Divorce Act s.2(2), a step-parent who stands in the place of a parent may have an obligation to pay child support; the amount is determined by the court considering all relevant circumstances, as the table amount may not reflect the appropriate level of step-parent support given that the biological parent may also have an obligation.
options.C: A step-parent is automatically equally liable for child support as the biological parent, and George must pay the full table amount.
options.D: George's child support obligation arises only if he formally adopted the child.

explanation: Under Divorce Act s.2(2), a person who stands in the place of a parent (in loco parentis) has obligations similar to those of a parent during the period they occupy that role. Federal Child Support Guidelines s.5 specifically addresses step-parent child support: where the 'spouse' (in the DA context, a person who stands in place of a parent) is obligated to pay support, the court may award an amount that the court considers appropriate having regard to the Guidelines and any other parent's legal duty to support the child. The court balances the step-parent's resources against the biological parent's obligation, often resulting in a lesser amount than the full table amount. Six years of acting as a de facto parent supports a finding that George stands in the place of a parent.
why_A_wrong: The Divorce Act and FCSG expressly recognize obligations of persons who stand in the place of a parent (step-parents in the de facto parental role). Legal obligation is not limited to biological or adoptive parents.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FCSG s.5 and DA s.2(2) recognize step-parent child support obligations where the step-parent has stood in the place of a parent; the amount is determined by the court considering all circumstances.
why_C_wrong: Step-parents are not automatically equally liable as biological parents at the full table amount. FCSG s.5 directs the court to determine an 'appropriate' amount having regard to the Guidelines and the biological parent's obligations — typically resulting in a lesser amount.
why_D_wrong: Formal adoption is not required to establish a step-parent obligation. A person who has functioned as a parent in a de facto sense for a significant period stands in the place of a parent under DA s.2(2) without the need for formal adoption.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Step-Parent Child Support — FCSG s.5 and DA s.2(2)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 5; Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 2(2)

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Income Above $150,000 — FCSG s.4 Departure from Table Amount
fact_pattern: Dr. Sharma earns $500,000 per year as a surgeon. He and his former spouse have two children aged 8 and 11 who live primarily with the mother. Under the Ontario Schedule I table, the table amount for a $150,000 income with two children is $1,980/month. Dr. Sharma argues that the court must award the table amount at the $150,000 benchmark since the table does not extend beyond $150,000. The mother argues the court should award more.
call_of_question: How does FCSG s.4 address child support where the payor's annual income exceeds $150,000?

options.A: The table amount at $150,000 is the maximum; a payor earning more than $150,000 pays no more than the table amount at that income level.
options.B: Under FCSG s.4, where the payor's income exceeds $150,000, the court has two options: (a) award the table amount for $150,000 plus an additional amount the court considers appropriate; or (b) if the table amount for $150,000 does not appropriately reflect the children's needs and the payor's ability to pay, award any amount it considers appropriate.
options.C: For income above $150,000, child support is automatically capped at 2% of income regardless of the table amount.
options.D: The court must apply the full proportional table formula by extrapolating beyond $150,000 as if the table extended indefinitely.

explanation: Federal Child Support Guidelines s.4 provides that where the payor's income exceeds $150,000, the court shall award: (a) the amount shown in the table for the $150,000 income level, plus an additional amount the court considers appropriate; or (b) if the table amount for $150,000 does not appropriately reflect the condition, means, needs, and circumstances of the children and the payor's ability to pay, any amount that the court considers appropriate. The SSAG for high-income payors recognizes that the table amounts for lower incomes may not adequately reflect what is appropriate when the payor has very high means. In Dr. Sharma's case, a court would likely award substantially more than the $1,980 table amount for $150,000.
why_A_wrong: FCSG s.4 does not cap child support at the $150,000 table amount for high-income payors. The court has discretion under s.4 to award additional amounts above the $150,000 benchmark.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FCSG s.4 provides two options for income above $150,000: table amount plus additional appropriate amount, or a fully discretionary amount if the table amount is not appropriate.
why_C_wrong: There is no 2% income cap formula in the Federal Child Support Guidelines. The FCSG does not contain such a provision for high-income payors.
why_D_wrong: The table does not extend beyond $150,000; the court does not extrapolate. Instead, FCSG s.4 provides the two-option framework for incomes above $150,000.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: High-Income Payor — FCSG s.4
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 4
